plainMeaning: Eminent people in the world who have perfected the mantra of your name-syllables drive deadly snake-venom far away and treat it as harmlessly as a tiny worm.
recitation: mannanti kīḍa-sarisaṁ, dūra paricchūḍha visama visavegā | tuha nāmakkhara phuḍasiddha, manta guruā narā loe ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mannanti < Skt. manyante, present 3rd pl. of √man 'to think, regard'
wordByWord.notes: The unstated object is the venom of the previous verse's cobra; the subject is narā ('men') in line 2.

wordByWord.gloss: like a worm / on a par with an insect
wordByWord.etymology: kīḍa (Skt. kīṭa, 'worm, insect') + sarisa (Skt. sadṛśa, 'similar to'); accusative singular agreeing with the implied venom
wordByWord.notes: The hyperbole: the serpent's lethal venom is downgraded to something as negligible as a crawling grub.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. dūra, 'far'; here adverbial, modifying paricchūḍha
wordByWord.notes: Reads as a unit with the following word: 'cast far off'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit past participle, pari- (around/away) + a √kṣubh/√chṛd base ('to expel, eject'); Skt. sense parikṣipta / nirasta
wordByWord.notes: Bahuvrīhi force: 'those by whom (the venom) has been driven far away,' qualifying narā.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. viṣama, 'uneven, dire, dangerous'; modifies visavegā
wordByWord.notes: Pun-adjacent to visa ('poison') one word later, sharpening the menace.

wordByWord.gloss: the rush / onset-force of the venom
wordByWord.etymology: visa (Skt. viṣa, 'poison') + vega (Skt. vega, 'speed, violent force'); the surging spread of venom through the body
wordByWord.notes: Object of paricchūḍha — it is this 'poison-rush' that is hurled away.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit genitive of the 2nd person, Skt. tava; addressed to Pārśvanātha

wordByWord.gloss: name-syllables (the very letters of your name)
wordByWord.etymology: nāma (Skt. nāman, 'name') + akkhara (Skt. akṣara, 'syllable, imperishable letter')
wordByWord.notes: The akṣaras themselves, not merely the name's sense, are treated as efficacious.

wordByWord.etymology: phuḍa (Skt. sphuṭa, 'clear, manifest') + siddha (Skt. siddha, 'accomplished, proven, potent'); a 'demonstrably perfected' formula
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies manta — a mantra whose power needs no external proof.
Predicated of tuha nāmakkhara: 'your name-syllables ARE a manifestly-perfected mantra.'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit guruā < Skt. gurukāḥ, 'great, weighty, venerable'; nominative plural qualifying narā
wordByWord.notes: Not 'teachers' here but 'men of weight/stature' — those grounded in the perfected name-mantra.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. narāḥ, nominative plural of nara, 'man'
Prakrit locative loe < Skt. loke, from loka, 'world'
wordByWord.notes: Locates the claim in lived human experience, not myth.

literalTranslation: Those weighty men in the world for whom the syllables of your name are a manifestly-perfected mantra — having driven far off the terrible rush of venom — regard it as no more than a worm.
idiomaticTranslation: In this world, the great souls for whom the very letters of your name have become a proven, living mantra hurl the deadly surge of venom far away — and reckon it as trifling as a crawling worm.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the 'deliverance' half of the serpent couplet whose terror was painted just before: the hooded, flame-eyed, flickering-tongued cobra of v.8 is here neutralised, and the snake's most lethal property — the racing spread of its venom (visa-vega) — is what gets 'cast far off' (dūra paricchūḍha). The poet's pivot is precise and devotional: deliverance is credited not to an antidote or a charmer's art but to tuha nāmakkhara — the syllables of Pārśva's name. Two compounds carry the theology. Phuḍa-siddha ('manifestly perfected') insists the name-mantra is not a formula awaiting validation; its potency is already self-evident, attested 'in the world' (loe) by those who wield it. And guruā ('the weighty ones') describes the reciters: people made spiritually substantial by that very mantra, so that venom which would fell ordinary flesh registers to them as kīḍa-sarisaṁ — on a par with a harmless grub. The comparison is deliberately bathetic: the most feared poison shrunk to the most ignorable creature. Notice the verse never narrates a snakebite cured; it states a settled disposition — such people simply 'regard' (mannanti) venom as trivial, because dread itself has already been dissolved at the root. That is the hymn's signature logic — fear, not merely its occasion, is what the name removes — applied here to its sharpest test-case, the serpent. The verse thus advances the litany from the cobra of v.8 toward the next peril, the bandits of the wilderness in v.10.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse crystallises nāma-smaraṇa raised to mantra-status: the akṣaras of a Jina's name are themselves akṣara — 'imperishable' — and so function as a siddha-mantra, the doctrine that the Tīrthaṅkara's name participates in his fear-conquering power. There is also a quiet ethical register: viṣa is, across Jain devotional idiom, a standing figure for the venom of the passions (kaṣāya), so 'driving the poison-rush far off' reads simultaneously as immunity to literal snakebite and as the practitioner's inner equanimity, in which the agitations that poison the soul are reduced to insignificance.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter meets this verse as a training in proportion: whatever 'venom' looms — illness, threat, or the inner poison of anger and fear — the practice is to anchor in the name until the menace shrinks to 'worm-sized.' It teaches that steady remembrance reorders one's estimate of danger, so that composure, not panic, becomes the reflex.

crossReferences.text: Uvasaggaharaṁ Stotra (4th Smaraṇa), on Pārśva and the neutralising of viṣa (visahara visa-nināsa)
Namiūṇa Bhayahara Stotra v.18 (the summary verse listing serpent/poison among the dangers stilled by Pārśva's name)
crossReferences.source: Mānatuṅgasūri (trad.), Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Namiūṇa Bhayahara Stotra v.23 (the hidden eighteen-syllable mantra), on the name-as-mantra theme this verse anticipates

sources.title: Parshvanatha — 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, serpent iconography and the Kamaṭha/Dharaṇendra legend
sources.consultedFor: identity of the addressee and the snake/venom associations underpinning the serpent couplet (vv.8–9)

sources.title: Manatunga (Jain ācārya)
sources.consultedFor: traditional authorship of the fear-removing Pārśva/Ṛṣabha stotras, corroborating the preface's attribution

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: root-glosses for viṣa, vega, kīṭa, sadṛśa, sphuṭa, siddha, mantra, akṣara, manyate underlying the per-token IAST etymologies

notes: Verse text follows the supplied Gujarati-script reading verbatim (e.g. પરિચ્છૂઢ, ફુડસિદ્ધ, ગુરુઆ); other recensions print variants such as paricchūḍha/parichaḍiya and nāvakkhara, which shift nothing essential. Two parsings of paricchūḍha-visama-visavegā are viable: (a) read here as a bahuvrīhi describing narā ('men by whom the terrible venom-rush has been cast far off'), or (b) an absolutive-like 'having driven the venom far away'; the sense is identical. guruā is taken as 'weighty/eminent men' rather than 'gurus.' The implied object of mannanti ('venom') is supplied from v.8's serpent. The web-search engine paraphrased rather than quoting a critical edition; glosses for paricchūḍha and visa-vega were cross-checked against standard Skt. roots (Cologne), and should be treated as well-supported but not from a single verbatim Prakrit lexicon entry — flagged accordingly.
